title: Not All Memories Age the Same: Autodiscovery of Adaptive Decay in Knowledge Graphs
comment: 27 pages, 2 figures, 19 tables (including appendix). Preprint under review
summary:
Knowledge graphs used for retrieval treat all facts as equally current. Existing temporal approaches apply uniform decay, using a single forgetting curve regardless of knowledge type. We show this is fundamentally misspecified: different knowledge types exhibit different temporal dynamics, and the core retrieval problem is not latency or throughput but identifying what is important at query time.
  We propose a hierarchical framework that replaces uniform decay with a continuous decay surface parameterized by two orthogonal signals: velocity (how frequently a concept is observed) and volatility (how much the value changes between observations, measured via embedding distance). The decay surface is decomposed into three learnable levels: domain-level parameters capture universal patterns (some predicates are inherently permanent, others inherently transient), context-level parameters capture setting-dependent variation, and entity-level adaptation personalizes decay to specific subjects. All parameters emerge from data through survival analysis on observed value lifetimes, requiring no predefined taxonomies or domain expertise.
  We formulate edge lifetime as a survival problem where the event is value supersession (a meaningfully different value replacing the current one), distinct from mere re-observation. Experiments on synthetic temporal knowledge graphs demonstrate recovery of planted hierarchical parameters (HDBSCAN ARI = 1.0).
  Validation on 107 Wikipedia articles and 1,163 patient records from the Synthea clinical EHR simulator shows that velocity-volatility clusters emerge naturally, align with observable persistence patterns, and near-universally exhibit the Lindy effect (Weibull shape k < 1). Uniform decay performs 18x worse than no temporal weighting. Heterogeneous decay recovers from this, with each hierarchy level contributing measurable improvement.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes replacing uniform temporal decay in knowledge graph retrieval with a hierarchical, learnable decay surface based on velocity (frequency) and volatility (value change), demonstrating superior performance over uniform decay across synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing temporal knowledge graph methods apply uniform forgetting curves regardless of knowledge type, which is misspecified because different knowledge types exhibit different temporal dynamics and the real problem is identifying query-time importance rather than system performance.
AI.method: A hierarchical framework using a continuous decay surface parameterized by velocity (observation frequency) and volatility (embedding-distance-based value change), decomposed into domain-level, context-level, and entity-level learnable parameters via survival analysis on observed value lifetimes, where edge lifetime is formulated as a survival problem with value supersession as the event.
AI.result: Recovers planted hierarchical parameters perfectly (HDBSCAN ARI=1.0) on synthetic data; validated on 107 Wikipedia articles and 1,163 patient records, shows emergent velocity-volatility clusters, near-universal Lindy effect (Weibull k<1), and uniform decay performs 18x worse than no temporal weighting while heterogeneous decay recovers measurably at each hierarchy level.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge types have fundamentally different temporal dynamics; hierarchical heterogeneous decay significantly outperforms uniform decay, suggesting that retrieval systems should learn rather than assume forgetting patterns.

title: Better Models, Faster Training: Sigmoid Attention for single-cell Foundation Models
summary: Training stable biological foundation models requires rethinking attention mechanisms: we find that using sigmoid attention as a drop in replacement for softmax attention a) produces better learned representations: on six diverse single-cell datasets, sigmoid achieves 25% higher cell-type separation, better cell-type cohesion metrics, and lower validation loss, b) faster training, models with sigmoid attention train up to 10% faster than their softmax counterparts, and c) more stable training by eliminating inherent sources of instability in softmax attention. We establish that sigmoid attention has globally bounded derivatives ($\leq 0.25$) as opposed to softmax, and a diagonal Jacobian structure in contrast with softmax's dense coupling, which together help alleviate training instabilities. In stress tests on 160M-parameter bidirectional attention models trained without gradient clipping on 8K-token sequences, softmax diverges catastrophically, with gradients exploding by four orders of magnitude, while sigmoid remains stable. Finally, we implement and open-source TritonSigmoid, an efficient GPU kernel that achieves 515 TFLOPS on H100 GPUs, outperforming both FlashAttention-2 and FlashSigmoid, with native padding support, which is essential for biological sequences. Our results establish sigmoid attention as both theoretically grounded and empirically superior for biological foundation models. Code is available at https://github.com/MSDLLCpapers/triton-sigmoid

AI.tldr: Sigmoid attention replaces softmax attention in biological foundation models, achieving 25% better cell-type separation, 10% faster training, more stable training, and an efficient TritonSigmoid GPU kernel achieving 515 TFLOPS on H100 GPUs.
AI.motivation: Training stable biological foundation models requires rethinking attention mechanisms because softmax attention has inherent instability issues (exploding gradients, dense coupling) that hinder training of large-scale biological models.
AI.method: The authors propose using sigmoid attention as a drop-in replacement for softmax attention. They analyze theoretical properties (globally bounded derivatives ≤0.25, diagonal Jacobian structure vs. softmax's dense coupling). They also implement TritonSigmoid, an efficient GPU kernel with native padding support.
AI.result: On six single-cell datasets, sigmoid achieves 25% higher cell-type separation, better cohesion metrics, and lower validation loss. Models train up to 10% faster. In stress tests on 160M-parameter models with 8K-token sequences, softmax diverges with gradients exploding by 4 orders of magnitude while sigmoid remains stable. TritonSigmoid achieves 515 TFLOPS on H100 GPUs, outperforming FlashAttention-2 and FlashSigmoid.
AI.conclusion: Sigmoid attention is both theoretically grounded and empirically superior for biological foundation models, offering better representations, faster training, and greater stability.

title: Universal Nano-Bead Emitter Inks for Programmable Nanometric Fluorescent Architectures
summary: Fabricating brightly fluorescent layers with nanometric thickness and digitally controlled lateral structuration remains a challenge for next-generation photonic devices, optical calibration standards, and biocompatible interfaces. Here, we introduce Nano-Bead Emitters (NBEs), hydrogel nanoparticles covalently functionalized with fluorophores, as a universal, water-processable ink platform for fabricating programmable nanometric fluorescent architectures. By immobilizing fluorophores within a charged nanohydrogel scaffold, the platform entirely decouples film morphology from dye solubility. This molecule-independent strategy enables spectrally distinct, inherently water-insoluble dyes to be processed using a single, standardized aqueous ink formulation. Combined with laser-induced forward transfer (LIFT) printing, this additive approach yields highly uniform fluorescent layers (~7 nm thickness, sub-nanometric roughness). This structural invariance produces complex multicolor patterns sharing identical thickness and surface morphology across all spectral channels, a critical requirement for quantitative optical calibration. Furthermore, LIFT printing provides programmable, layer-by-layer control over fluorescence intensity via successive deposition cycles, yielding precisely tunable brightness without aggregation-caused quenching. This maskless technique enables rapid, high-fidelity printing of both monochromatic and multicolor patterns over macroscopic areas with absolute spatial resolution. Finally, these universally compatible NBE inks stably deposit onto diverse substrates (glass, polymers, semiconductors, metasurfaces), effectively bridging scalable manufacturing with high-performance integrated photonic systems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Nano-Bead Emitters (NBEs)—hydrogel nanoparticles with covalently attached fluorophores—as a universal, water-processable ink platform for printing nanometric fluorescent architectures. Combined with laser-induced forward transfer (LIFT) printing, it achieves ~7 nm thick, uniform fluorescent layers with tunable brightness, multicolor patterning, and substrate versatility without dye solubility issues.
AI.motivation: The challenge of fabricating brightly fluorescent layers with nanometric thickness and digitally controlled lateral structuration for next-generation photonic devices, optical calibration standards, and biocompatible interfaces.
AI.method: Nano-Bead Emitters (NBEs)—hydrogel nanoparticles covalently functionalized with fluorophores—are used as a universal aqueous ink platform. Combined with laser-induced forward transfer (LIFT) printing, this enables additive, maskless, layer-by-layer deposition.
AI.result: Uniform fluorescent layers (~7 nm thickness, sub-nanometric roughness); decoupled film morphology from dye solubility; multicolor patterns with identical thickness/morphology; tunable brightness via successive deposition without aggregation quenching; high-fidelity printing over macroscopic areas; stable deposition on diverse substrates (glass, polymers, semiconductors, metasurfaces).
AI.conclusion: The NBE ink platform effectively bridges scalable manufacturing with high-performance integrated photonic systems, offering a universal, water-processable solution for programmable nanometric fluorescent architectures.

title: Not All Memories Age the Same: Autodiscovery of Adaptive Decay in Knowledge Graphs
comment: 27 pages, 2 figures, 19 tables (including appendix). Preprint under review
summary:
Knowledge graphs used for retrieval treat all facts as equally current. Existing temporal approaches apply uniform decay, using a single forgetting curve regardless of knowledge type. We show this is fundamentally misspecified: different knowledge types exhibit different temporal dynamics, and the core retrieval problem is not latency or throughput but identifying what is important at query time.
  We propose a hierarchical framework that replaces uniform decay with a continuous decay surface parameterized by two orthogonal signals: velocity (how frequently a concept is observed) and volatility (how much the value changes between observations, measured via embedding distance). The decay surface is decomposed into three learnable levels: domain-level parameters capture universal patterns (some predicates are inherently permanent, others inherently transient), context-level parameters capture setting-dependent variation, and entity-level adaptation personalizes decay to specific subjects. All parameters emerge from data through survival analysis on observed value lifetimes, requiring no predefined taxonomies or domain expertise.
  We formulate edge lifetime as a survival problem where the event is value supersession (a meaningfully different value replacing the current one), distinct from mere re-observation. Experiments on synthetic temporal knowledge graphs demonstrate recovery of planted hierarchical parameters (HDBSCAN ARI = 1.0).
  Validation on 107 Wikipedia articles and 1,163 patient records from the Synthea clinical EHR simulator shows that velocity-volatility clusters emerge naturally, align with observable persistence patterns, and near-universally exhibit the Lindy effect (Weibull shape k < 1). Uniform decay performs 18x worse than no temporal weighting. Heterogeneous decay recovers from this, with each hierarchy level contributing measurable improvement.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes replacing uniform temporal decay in knowledge graph retrieval with a hierarchical, learnable decay surface based on velocity and volatility signals, demonstrating significant improvements over uniform decay on synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Current knowledge graph retrieval treats all facts as equally current, and existing temporal approaches apply a single forgetting curve regardless of knowledge type. This is misspecified because different knowledge types exhibit different temporal dynamics, and the core problem is identifying what is important at query time—not latency or throughput.
AI.method: The paper replaces uniform decay with a continuous decay surface parameterized by velocity (frequency of concept observation) and volatility (magnitude of value change between observations, measured via embedding distance). This surface is decomposed into three learnable hierarchy levels: domain-level parameters for universal patterns, context-level parameters for setting-dependent variation, and entity-level adaptation. All parameters are learned from data through survival analysis on observed value lifetimes. Edge lifetime is formulated as a survival problem where the event is value supersession.
AI.result: 1) Synthetic experiments demonstrate recovery of planted hierarchical parameters (HDBSCAN ARI = 1.0). 2) On 107 Wikipedia articles and 1,163 Synthea patient records, velocity-volatility clusters emerge naturally, align with observable persistence patterns, and exhibit the Lindy effect (Weibull shape k < 1). 3) Uniform decay performs 18x worse than no temporal weighting. 4) Heterogeneous decay recovers from this, with each hierarchy level contributing measurable improvement.
AI.conclusion: Uniform temporal decay is fundamentally flawed for knowledge graph retrieval. A hierarchical, data-driven approach using velocity and volatility signals significantly outperforms uniform decay, and each level of the hierarchy contributes measurable improvement. The Lindy effect is near-universally observed in real-world data.

title: Better Models, Faster Training: Sigmoid Attention for single-cell Foundation Models
summary: Training stable biological foundation models requires rethinking attention mechanisms: we find that using sigmoid attention as a drop in replacement for softmax attention a) produces better learned representations: on six diverse single-cell datasets, sigmoid achieves 25% higher cell-type separation, better cell-type cohesion metrics, and lower validation loss, b) faster training, models with sigmoid attention train up to 10% faster than their softmax counterparts, and c) more stable training by eliminating inherent sources of instability in softmax attention. We establish that sigmoid attention has globally bounded derivatives ($\leq 0.25$) as opposed to softmax, and a diagonal Jacobian structure in contrast with softmax's dense coupling, which together help alleviate training instabilities. In stress tests on 160M-parameter bidirectional attention models trained without gradient clipping on 8K-token sequences, softmax diverges catastrophically, with gradients exploding by four orders of magnitude, while sigmoid remains stable. Finally, we implement and open-source TritonSigmoid, an efficient GPU kernel that achieves 515 TFLOPS on H100 GPUs, outperforming both FlashAttention-2 and FlashSigmoid, with native padding support, which is essential for biological sequences. Our results establish sigmoid attention as both theoretically grounded and empirically superior for biological foundation models. Code is available at https://github.com/MSDLLCpapers/triton-sigmoid

AI.tldr: Sigmoid attention outperforms softmax attention as a drop-in replacement for biological foundation models, achieving 25% better cell-type separation, 10% faster training, and more stable training due to globally bounded derivatives and diagonal Jacobian structure. They also open-source TritonSigmoid, an efficient GPU kernel achieving 515 TFLOPS on H100s.
AI.motivation: Training stable biological foundation models requires rethinking attention mechanisms to address inherent instabilities in softmax attention (e.g., gradient explosion, dense coupling).
AI.method: Replace softmax attention with sigmoid attention as a drop-in replacement; analyze theoretical properties (globally bounded derivatives ≤0.25, diagonal Jacobian structure); implement TritonSigmoid GPU kernel for efficiency.
AI.result: Sigmoid achieves 25% higher cell-type separation, better cohesion metrics, lower validation loss, up to 10% faster training, and stable training on 160M-parameter models with 8K-token sequences where softmax diverges. TritonSigmoid achieves 515 TFLOPS on H100, outperforming FlashAttention-2 and FlashSigmoid.
AI.conclusion: Sigmoid attention is both theoretically grounded and empirically superior for biological foundation models.

title: Universal Nano-Bead Emitter Inks for Programmable Nanometric Fluorescent Architectures
summary: Fabricating brightly fluorescent layers with nanometric thickness and digitally controlled lateral structuration remains a challenge for next-generation photonic devices, optical calibration standards, and biocompatible interfaces. Here, we introduce Nano-Bead Emitters (NBEs), hydrogel nanoparticles covalently functionalized with fluorophores, as a universal, water-processable ink platform for fabricating programmable nanometric fluorescent architectures. By immobilizing fluorophores within a charged nanohydrogel scaffold, the platform entirely decouples film morphology from dye solubility. This molecule-independent strategy enables spectrally distinct, inherently water-insoluble dyes to be processed using a single, standardized aqueous ink formulation. Combined with laser-induced forward transfer (LIFT) printing, this additive approach yields highly uniform fluorescent layers (~7 nm thickness, sub-nanometric roughness). This structural invariance produces complex multicolor patterns sharing identical thickness and surface morphology across all spectral channels, a critical requirement for quantitative optical calibration. Furthermore, LIFT printing provides programmable, layer-by-layer control over fluorescence intensity via successive deposition cycles, yielding precisely tunable brightness without aggregation-caused quenching. This maskless technique enables rapid, high-fidelity printing of both monochromatic and multicolor patterns over macroscopic areas with absolute spatial resolution. Finally, these universally compatible NBE inks stably deposit onto diverse substrates (glass, polymers, semiconductors, metasurfaces), effectively bridging scalable manufacturing with high-performance integrated photonic systems.

AI.tldr: Nano-Bead Emitters (NBEs) are hydrogel nanoparticles functionalized with fluorophores that serve as a universal, water-processable ink platform for printing nanometric fluorescent layers with precise thickness control, multicolor capability, and compatibility across diverse substrates.
AI.motivation: Fabricating brightly fluorescent layers with nanometric thickness and digitally controlled lateral structuration remains challenging for next-generation photonic devices, optical calibration standards, and biocompatible interfaces.
AI.method: The authors introduce Nano-Bead Emitters (NBEs)—hydrogel nanoparticles covalently functionalized with fluorophores within a charged nanohydrogel scaffold, combined with laser-induced forward transfer (LIFT) printing for additive, maskless fabrication.
AI.result: The approach yields highly uniform fluorescent layers (~7 nm thickness, sub-nanometric roughness), enables complex multicolor patterns with identical morphology across spectral channels, and provides programmable brightness control via successive deposition cycles without aggregation-caused quenching.
AI.conclusion: NBE inks are universally compatible and stably deposit onto diverse substrates (glass, polymers, semiconductors, metasurfaces), effectively bridging scalable manufacturing with high-performance integrated photonic systems.

title: T-cell repertoire response in individuals with post-acute sequelae of COVID-19
summary: T-cells are central to SARS-CoV-2 clearance and immunological memory, yet their contribution to the persistence of post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC) remains poorly understood. The immunological features that distinguish individuals who develop PASC from those who recover fully are unresolved, in part due to the phenotypic heterogeneity of the condition and the likely multiplicity of its underlying mechanisms. Here, we profiled longitudinal bulk TCR$β$ repertoires from 120 individuals in the INCOV cohort--71 with PASC and 49 without--sampled at two to three time points spanning the acute and post-acute phases of infection. Using robust statistical modeling of repertoire composition and clonal dynamics, we found that global statistics such as V, J gene usage and CDR3 length do not differ between groups, but that locally enriched sequence motifs and differentially dynamic clones reveal distinct T-cell signatures associated with PASC status. Clones contracting following the peak of the acute response were significantly enriched for SARS-CoV-2 specificity in both groups. Interestingly, Influenza A-specific TCRs were disproportionately enriched among contracting clones in PASC{$^+$} repertoires, implicating viral co-infection as a potential contributor to early disease severity and, possibly, PASC pathogenesis. Rare public TCR clones were markedly enriched for SARS-CoV-2 specificity, with PASC{$^+$} individuals harboring a modestly but significantly higher proportion than PASC{$^-$} individuals. Together, we identified over 1,000 candidate TCR$β$ receptors potentially discriminating PASC{$^+$} from PASC{$^-$} immune responses, opening a path toward the identification of disease-relevant T-cell specificities and the development of T-cell-based immunological biomarkers for long COVID.

AI.tldr: This study profiled longitudinal TCRβ repertoires from 120 COVID-19 patients (71 with PASC/long COVID, 49 without) and found distinct T-cell signatures—including Influenza A-specific TCRs enriched among contracting clones in PASC patients—suggesting viral co-infection may contribute to PASC pathogenesis, and identified over 1,000 candidate TCRβ receptors that could serve as biomarkers for long COVID.
AI.motivation: To understand why some individuals develop post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC/long COVID) while others fully recover, specifically by investigating the role of T-cell responses, which are central to SARS-CoV-2 clearance but whose contribution to PASC persistence remains poorly understood.
AI.method: Profiled longitudinal bulk TCRβ repertoires from 120 individuals (71 PASC+, 49 PASC-) in the INCOV cohort at 2-3 time points across acute and post-acute phases, using robust statistical modeling of repertoire composition, clonal dynamics, locally enriched sequence motifs, and differentially dynamic clones.
AI.result: Global statistics (V/J gene usage, CDR3 length) did not differ between groups, but locally enriched sequence motifs and clonal dynamics revealed distinct T-cell signatures. Influenza A-specific TCRs were disproportionately enriched among contracting clones in PASC+ repertoires. Rare public TCR clones showed higher SARS-CoV-2 specificity in PASC+ individuals.
AI.conclusion: Identified over 1,000 candidate TCRβ receptors potentially discriminating PASC+ from PASC- immune responses, opening a path toward identifying disease-relevant T-cell specificities and developing T-cell-based immunological biomarkers for long COVID.

title: Entropy-Dominated Temporal Vocal Dynamics as Digital Biomarkers for Depression Detection
summary: Automated depression detection often relies on static aggregation of conversational signals, potentially obscuring clinically meaningful behavioral dynamics. We investigated whether entropy-driven temporal biomarkers improve depression detection beyond standard pooled features using the DAIC-WOZ corpus. Using 142 labeled participants, we reconstructed utterance-level acoustic trajectories and compared pooled temporal baselines, trajectory dynamics, Shannon entropy biomarkers, recurrence quantification, sample entropy, fractal complexity, and coupling biomarkers under leakage-aware validation. Static pooling achieved an AUC of 0.593, trajectory dynamics improved performance to 0.637, and entropy biomarkers produced the strongest statistically significant improvement over pooled baselines (AUC 0.646; nested cross-validated AUC 0.615; permutation p = 0.017). Entropy biomarkers outperformed recurrence, coupling, sample entropy, and fractalbased features, with several biomarkers stable across folds. These findings suggest depression-related signal may lie less in average acoustic levels than in entropy of conversational dynamics, supporting temporally informed digital phenotypes for mental-health assessment.

AI.tldr: Entropy-driven temporal biomarkers improve depression detection (AUC 0.646) over static pooled features (AUC 0.593) using acoustic trajectories from the DAIC-WOZ corpus.
AI.motivation: Automated depression detection typically relies on static aggregation of conversational signals, potentially missing clinically meaningful behavioral dynamics over time.
AI.method: Using 142 labeled participants from DAIC-WOZ, utterance-level acoustic trajectories were reconstructed and compared across pooled temporal baselines, trajectory dynamics, Shannon entropy, recurrence quantification, sample entropy, fractal complexity, and coupling biomarkers under leakage-aware validation.
AI.result: Static pooling AUC 0.593; trajectory dynamics AUC 0.637; entropy biomarkers achieved best performance (AUC 0.646; nested cross-validated AUC 0.615; permutation p=0.017), outperforming recurrence, coupling, sample entropy, and fractal-based features.
AI.conclusion: Depression-related signal lies more in entropy of conversational dynamics than in average acoustic levels, supporting temporally informed digital phenotypes for mental-health assessment.

title: Modeling the impact of host diversity on the evolution of vector feeding preferences and implications for disease control
summary: Vector-borne diseases often infect multiple host species, increasing the likelihood of disease persistence due to the presence of multiple reservoirs. Vector biting patterns and feeding preferences can shift in response to selective pressures introduced by disease control interventions, altering the dynamics of transmission. In this paper, we develop a mathematical model that couples host diversity and adaptive vector behavior with vector-borne disease transmission dynamics, focusing on a system with two hosts and a vector population exhibiting preference for one host. We derive the basic reproduction number, $R_0$, a threshold that determines the existence of two equilibria in our model, and obtain conditions that can lead to the long-term persistence of the disease. Our analysis suggests that shortening the infectious period of the vector's preferred host is an effective control strategy. We also identified a threshold that determines whether shifting vector preference toward a non-preferred host amplifies or reduces the disease burden on the primary preferred host. Our results show that protective measures for the preferred host can trigger adaptive shifts in vector preferences, reducing disease prevalence in that host. However, this shift may lead to an increase in overall host prevalence.

AI.tldr: A mathematical model shows that vector-borne diseases with multiple hosts and adaptive vector feeding behavior can persist; shortening the infectious period of the preferred host is effective, but protective measures may shift vector preferences and increase overall disease prevalence.
AI.motivation: Vector-borne diseases often infect multiple host species, and vector biting preferences can shift adaptively under disease control interventions, yet how host diversity and adaptive vector behavior jointly affect transmission dynamics is not well understood.
AI.method: Develop a mathematical model coupling two-host, one-vector system with adaptive vector preference; derive basic reproduction number R0 and equilibrium conditions.
AI.result: Shortening the infectious period of the preferred host is an effective control strategy; a threshold determines whether shifting vector preference toward a non-preferred host amplifies or reduces disease burden on the preferred host; protective measures for the preferred host can reduce its prevalence but may increase overall prevalence.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive vector behavior complicates disease control; interventions targeting the preferred host may shift vector preferences and alter overall transmission dynamics, requiring careful assessment of both intended and unintended consequences.

title: Epidemic Extinction in a Continuous SIRS Model with Vaccination
summary: Epidemics have shaped human history, often with devastating consequences, motivating the development of mathematical models to understand and control their dynamics. Among the many aspects of epidemic behavior, the conditions that lead to epidemic extinction stand out as a central-if not the fundamental-question in epidemic modeling. In this work, we study epidemic extinction in a continuous SIRS (Susceptible-Infected-Recovered-Susceptible) model governed by a system of ordinary differential equations (ODEs). The model includes vaccination as a time-dependent process and considers the reinfection of recovered individuals through waning immunity. We analyze how different parameter regimes -- particularly infection, recovery, and immunity loss rates -- affect the persistence or extinction of the epidemic. Special attention is given to the limitations of continuous population models, in which the infected fraction can fall below the equivalent of a single individual, leading to nonphysical outcomes such as unrealistically long persistence or artificial secondary peaks. By comparing the continuous SIRS dynamics with expected real-world thresholds for extinction, we highlight the importance of incorporating stochasticity or discrete effects to accurately describe epidemic fade-out.

AI.tldr: This paper studies epidemic extinction in a continuous SIRS model with vaccination and waning immunity, analyzing how infection, recovery, and immunity loss rates affect persistence or extinction, while highlighting limitations of continuous models that produce nonphysical outcomes below single-individual thresholds.
AI.motivation: Understanding the conditions that lead to epidemic extinction is a fundamental question in epidemic modeling, and the authors aim to address limitations of continuous population models that can produce unrealistic results when infected fractions fall below single-individual equivalents.
AI.method: The authors analyze a continuous SIRS model governed by ODEs, incorporating time-dependent vaccination and waning immunity (reinfection). They examine different parameter regimes of infection, recovery, and immunity loss rates to study epidemic persistence versus extinction.
AI.result: Continuous population models exhibit nonphysical outcomes such as unrealistically long persistence or artificial secondary peaks when the infected fraction falls below the equivalent of a single individual, highlighting discrepancies between model predictions and real-world extinction thresholds.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating stochasticity or discrete effects is essential for accurately describing epidemic fade-out, as continuous models have fundamental limitations in capturing extinction dynamics near the single-individual threshold.

title: Personalizing Cancer Models under Data Scarcity via Parameter Decomposition
summary: Personalized cancer modeling for clinical applications requires robust and efficient parameter calibration, particularly in settings with limited patient data. This need is especially critical for medical digital twins (MDTs), which are virtual representations of disease continuously updated using longitudinal patient measurements. In this work, we propose a novel parameter personalization framework for dynamical cancer models under data scarcity. Our approach decomposes selected model parameters into a common component, shared across patients, and a personalized component, which is patient-specific and can be updated as new data become available. The common component captures population-level structure and is estimated once, providing an informed prior that enables rapid and accurate personalization. We demonstrate the effectiveness of this framework using synthetic data generated from canonical dynamical systems, such as logistic growth models with optimized treatment interventions. Our results show that parameter decomposition significantly improves calibration performance in limited-data regimes, facilitating fast and reliable personalization and supporting the development of patient-specific cancer models and MDTs.

AI.tldr: Proposes a parameter personalization framework for dynamical cancer models that decomposes parameters into shared (population-level) and patient-specific components to enable rapid, accurate calibration under data scarcity for medical digital twins.
AI.motivation: Personalized cancer modeling requires robust parameter calibration with limited patient data, which is critical for medical digital twins (MDTs) that rely on continuous updates from longitudinal measurements.
AI.method: Decomposes model parameters into a common component (shared across patients, estimated once) and a personalized component (patient-specific, updated as new data arrives), providing an informed prior for efficient personalization.
AI.result: Using synthetic data from logistic growth models with optimized treatment interventions, the parameter decomposition framework significantly improves calibration performance in limited-data regimes.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables fast and reliable personalization under data scarcity, supporting the development of patient-specific cancer models and medical digital twins.

title: Incorporating the underuse problem in the tragedy of the commons
summary: The tragedy of the commons has traditionally been framed as a problem of resource overuse driven by self-interested exploitation. In contrast, growing empirical evidence shows that insufficient use or abandonment of natural resources, known as underuse, can also lead to ecological degradation and loss of ecosystem services. Despite its relevance, underuse has rarely been examined within evolutionary theories of resource use. Here, we develop a simple eco-evolutionary model that integrates both provisioning and non-provisioning ecosystem services to analyze the evolution of resource-use strategies. Using adaptive dynamics, we investigate how individual resource use evolves while altering resource abundance. The model shows that overuse and underuse arise naturally as alternative evolutionary outcomes of the same underlying process, alongside intermediate use and evolutionary branching. We derive analytical conditions for the existence, number, and stability of evolutionarily singular strategies, and show that the qualitative evolutionary fate is primarily determined by the shape of provisioning benefits. Only when provisioning benefits increase in a concave manner does evolutionary dynamics converge to a unique intermediate strategy that is continuously stable. In contrast, convex increasing benefits generate a broader range of outcomes: overuse, underuse, bi-stability, and evolutionary branching. By explicitly comparing the continuously stable strategy with the socially optimal strategy, we further quantify how their deviations depend on the valuation of non-provisioning services. Our results provide a theoretical framework for viewing the common-pool resource dilemmas as intrinsically two-sided evolutionary problems, and offer a baseline for future studies exploring interventions to address overuse and underuse simultaneously.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an eco-evolutionary model showing that both overuse and underuse of common-pool resources can arise as alternative evolutionary outcomes depending on the shape of provisioning benefits, with convex benefits leading to diverse outcomes like overuse, underuse, bi-stability, and branching, while concave benefits converge to a stable intermediate strategy.
AI.motivation: While the tragedy of the commons is traditionally framed as resource overuse, growing empirical evidence shows underuse also causes ecological degradation. However, underuse has rarely been examined within evolutionary theories of resource use.
AI.method: The authors develop a simple eco-evolutionary model integrating provisioning and non-provisioning ecosystem services, using adaptive dynamics to analyze how individual resource-use strategies evolve while altering resource abundance.
AI.result: The model shows overuse and underuse emerge as alternative evolutionary outcomes. Concave provisioning benefits lead to a unique stable intermediate strategy, while convex benefits produce overuse, underuse, bi-stability, and evolutionary branching. Deviations from social optimum depend on valuation of non-provisioning services.
AI.conclusion: Common-pool resource dilemmas are intrinsically two-sided evolutionary problems requiring simultaneous consideration of overuse and underuse. The framework offers a baseline for interventions addressing both issues.

title: Simulating Infant First-Person Sensorimotor Experience via Motion Retargeting from Babies to Humanoids
comment: Submitted to IEEE ICDL. 8 pages, 6 figures
summary: Motion retargeting from humans to human-like artificial agents is becoming increasingly important as humanoid robots grow more capable. However, most existing approaches focus only on reproducing kinematics and ignore the rich sensorimotor experience associated with human movement. In this work, we present a framework for simulating the multimodal sensorimotor experiences of infants using physical and virtual humanoids. From a single video, our method reconstructs the infant's body configuration by extracting its skeletal structure and estimating the full 3D pose from each frame. Then we map the reconstructed motion onto several developmental platforms: the physical iCub robot and the virtual simulators pyCub, EMFANT and MIMo. Replaying the retargeted motions on these embodiments produces simulated multisensory streams including proprioception (joints and muscles), touch, and vision. For the best-matching embodiment, the retargeting achieves sub-centimeter accuracy and enables a rich multimodal analysis of infant development as well as enhanced automated annotation of behaviors. This framework provides a unique window into the infant's sensorimotor experience, offering new tools for robotics, developmental science, and early detection of neurodevelopmental disorders. The code is available at https://github.com/ctu-vras/motion-retargeting/.

AI.tldr: A framework that reconstructs infant motion from a single video and retargets it onto physical/virtual humanoid platforms (iCub, pyCub, EMFANT, MIMo), producing multimodal sensory streams (proprioception, touch, vision) with sub-centimeter accuracy for the best-matching embodiment.
AI.motivation: Existing motion retargeting approaches focus only on reproducing kinematics and ignore the rich sensorimotor experiences (proprioception, touch, vision) associated with human movement, limiting insights into infant development.
AI.method: Extracts infant skeletal structure and estimates full 3D pose from a single video frame-by-frame, then maps the reconstructed motion onto multiple embodiments (physical iCub robot, virtual pyCub, EMFANT, MIMo) to simulate multisensory streams.
AI.result: Achieves sub-centimeter accuracy for the best-matching embodiment; enables rich multimodal analysis of infant behavior and enhanced automated annotation.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a unique window into infant sensorimotor experience, offering new tools for robotics, developmental science, and early detection of neurodevelopmental disorders.

title: Benchmarking virtual cell models for in-the-wild perturbation response
summary: Virtual cell (VC) models aim to predict cellular responses to any perturbations in silico and have emerged as a promising approach for drug discovery and precision medicine. Yet, a clear gap still remains: while models routinely reported impressive results on standard benchmarks, it is unclear whether their predictions are truly meaningful in practice. This is mainly due to limitations in current evaluation setups, which are often overly simplified or inconsistent, and do not reflect the complexity and variability of real biological systems. Here, we introduce a standardized and modular benchmarking framework for virtual cell prediction. Our framework evaluates diverse models under in-the-wild challenging scenarios, including unseen cell contexts, unseen perturbations, and cross-dataset generalization, which better reflect practical applications. Our analysis shows that model performance is highly context-dependent and shaped by task design and evaluation criteria. In commonly used setups, performance is often overestimated, and naive dataset aggregation can even reduce performance. When evaluated under more strict conditions, model performance drops markedly, indicating limited robustness to shifts across cellular contexts. In unseen perturbation settings, models including simple linear approaches capture global transcriptional trends but fail to recover fine-grained perturbation-specific effects. In addition, different evaluation metrics focus on different biological properties, leading to substantially different model rankings. Together, our framework provides a more reliable and biologically grounded evaluation, offering clearer guidance for applying virtual cell models in real scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a standardized benchmarking framework for virtual cell models, revealing that current evaluation setups overestimate performance, and model predictions are highly context-dependent with limited robustness across cell contexts, unseen perturbations, and cross-dataset scenarios.
AI.motivation: Current virtual cell models report impressive benchmark results, but their practical utility is unclear due to oversimplified and inconsistent evaluation setups that fail to reflect the complexity of real biological systems.
AI.method: The authors develop a modular benchmarking framework evaluating models under challenging in-the-wild scenarios: unseen cell contexts, unseen perturbations, and cross-dataset generalization.
AI.result: Model performance is highly context-dependent and often overestimated in standard setups; naive dataset aggregation can reduce performance; under stricter conditions, performance drops markedly; even simple linear models capture global trends but miss fine-grained perturbation effects; different metrics yield different model rankings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a more reliable, biologically grounded evaluation, offering clearer guidance for deploying virtual cell models in real-world drug discovery and precision medicine applications.

title: On Agentic Behavioral Modeling
summary: Integrating theoretical neuroscience, decision theory, and probabilistic inference offers a promising route to understanding human cognition, yet concrete methodological bridges between agentic AI models and behavioral data analysis remain formally underdeveloped. We advance this synthesis under the framework of agentic behavioral modeling (ABM), which treats artificial agents as latent, generative hypotheses about cognitive mechanisms and evaluates them by their statistical adequacy in explaining human behavior. After outlining its conceptual foundations, we apply the framework to two minimal laboratory paradigms: a binary perceptual contrast-discrimination task and a symmetric two-armed bandit learning task. We formalize each task-agent-data system as a joint probability model, derive explicit conditional log-likelihoods for behavioral inference, validate different model variants using model and parameter recovery simulations, and evaluate them in light of empirical data. Using these minimal examples, we provide an agent-centric interpretation of the psychometric function, derive optimal policies for both tasks, and show the equivalence between Rescorla-Wagner learning and Bayesian inference in symmetric bandits. More broadly, this work may serve as a conceptual and practical foundation for applying ABM to cognitive behavioral science.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes Agentic Behavioral Modeling (ABM) as a formal framework bridging AI agents and human behavioral data analysis, demonstrated through two minimal lab tasks with explicit likelihood-based inference and foundational theoretical results.
AI.motivation: Despite the promise of integrating neuroscience, decision theory, and probabilistic inference for understanding human cognition, concrete methodological bridges between agentic AI models and behavioral data analysis remain formally underdeveloped.
AI.method: The authors formalize each task-agent-data system as a joint probability model, derive explicit conditional log-likelihoods, validate model variants via recovery simulations, and evaluate against empirical data using two minimal paradigms: perceptual contrast-discrimination and symmetric two-armed bandit learning.
AI.result: They provide an agent-centric interpretation of the psychometric function, derive optimal policies for both tasks, and prove the equivalence between Rescorla-Wagner learning and Bayesian inference in symmetric bandits.
AI.conclusion: This work serves as a conceptual and practical foundation for applying ABM to cognitive behavioral science, enabling principled evaluation of AI agents as hypotheses about human cognitive mechanisms.

title: Complex Effects of Salt on Small-Angle X-ray Scattering of BSA Originate From the Interplay of Ions and Hydration Water
summary: Salts are an integral part of the environment for living systems and, therefore, understanding their effects on proteins and other biomolecules is of fundamental interest. Small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) of protein solutions can provide valuable information on salt effects, but extracting this information has been a significant challenge. For example, SAXS data of bovine serum albumin (BSA) at various salt concentrations were fit to three different spherical models. Here we combined the newly developed FMAPIq approach with explicit-solvent all-atom molecular dynamics simulations to show that the complex effects of salt on the SAXS of BSA originate from the interplay of ions and hydration water, leading to a general picture of protein-ion-water interactions.

AI.tldr: This paper uses a new FMAPIq approach combined with molecular dynamics simulations to explain how salt affects SAXS data of BSA proteins through interactions between ions and hydration water.
AI.motivation: Salts are essential in biological environments, but understanding their effects on proteins is challenging. Previous attempts to fit SAXS data of BSA to spherical models were inadequate.
AI.method: The authors combined the novel FMAPIq approach with explicit-solvent all-atom molecular dynamics simulations to analyze SAXS data of BSA at various salt concentrations.
AI.result: The complex salt effects on BSA SAXS patterns originate from the interplay between ions and hydration water.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a general picture of protein-ion-water interactions, offering a framework for understanding salt effects on biomolecular systems.

title: Multisensory learning recruits visual neurons into an olfactory memory engram
summary: Associating multiple sensory cues with a single experience or object is a fundamental process that improves object recognition and memory performance. However, neural mechanisms that bind sensory features during learning and augment memory expression are unknown. Here we demonstrate multisensory appetitive and aversive memory in Drosophila. Combining colours and odours improved memory performance, even when each sensory modality was tested alone. Temporal control of neuronal function revealed visually-selective mushroom body Kenyon Cells (KCs) to be required for enhancement of visual and olfactory memory recall after multisensory training. Synapse-level connectomics suggests that valence-relevant dopaminergic reinforcement could permit the KC-spanning serotonergic DPM neurons to bridge between previously modality-selective KC streams. Consistent with this model, DPM transmission is uniquely required during multisensory memory formation and for enhanced expression of olfactory memory afterwards. In addition, signalling via the DopR1 dopamine receptor is required in APL neurons, suggesting that reinforcing dopamine could locally release GABA-ergic inhibition to permit bridging microcircuits to function. Cross-modal binding thereby expands the KCs representing the olfactory memory engram into those representing the colour. We propose that broadening of the engram improves memory performance after multisensory learning and permits a single sensory feature to retrieve the memory of the multimodal experience.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals neural mechanisms of multisensory memory formation in Drosophila, showing that mushroom body Kenyon Cells, DPM neurons, and dopaminergic signaling enable cross-modal binding between visual and olfactory cues, broadening the memory engram and improving recall.
AI.motivation: To understand the neural mechanisms underlying multisensory binding during learning and how it enhances memory expression, which was previously unknown.
AI.method: Combined behavioral assays (multisensory appetitive/aversive conditioning in Drosophila) with temporal neuronal manipulation, synapse-level connectomics, and genetic approaches to dissect the circuit mechanisms.
AI.result: Visual KCs are required for enhanced memory after multisensory training; DPM neurons bridge modality-selective KC streams via dopaminergic reinforcement; DopR1 receptor in APL neurons modulates GABA-ergic inhibition to enable microcircuit function.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal binding expands the olfactory memory engram to include color-representing KCs, improving memory performance and allowing a single sensory feature to retrieve the multimodal memory.

title: Not All Memories Age the Same: Autodiscovery of Adaptive Decay in Knowledge Graphs
comment: 27 pages, 2 figures, 19 tables (including appendix). Preprint under review
summary:
Knowledge graphs used for retrieval treat all facts as equally current. Existing temporal approaches apply uniform decay, using a single forgetting curve regardless of knowledge type. We show this is fundamentally misspecified: different knowledge types exhibit different temporal dynamics, and the core retrieval problem is not latency or throughput but identifying what is important at query time.
  We propose a hierarchical framework that replaces uniform decay with a continuous decay surface parameterized by two orthogonal signals: velocity (how frequently a concept is observed) and volatility (how much the value changes between observations, measured via embedding distance). The decay surface is decomposed into three learnable levels: domain-level parameters capture universal patterns (some predicates are inherently permanent, others inherently transient), context-level parameters capture setting-dependent variation, and entity-level adaptation personalizes decay to specific subjects. All parameters emerge from data through survival analysis on observed value lifetimes, requiring no predefined taxonomies or domain expertise.
  We formulate edge lifetime as a survival problem where the event is value supersession (a meaningfully different value replacing the current one), distinct from mere re-observation. Experiments on synthetic temporal knowledge graphs demonstrate recovery of planted hierarchical parameters (HDBSCAN ARI = 1.0).
  Validation on 107 Wikipedia articles and 1,163 patient records from the Synthea clinical EHR simulator shows that velocity-volatility clusters emerge naturally, align with observable persistence patterns, and near-universally exhibit the Lindy effect (Weibull shape k < 1). Uniform decay performs 18x worse than no temporal weighting. Heterogeneous decay recovers from this, with each hierarchy level contributing measurable improvement.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a hierarchical framework for temporal knowledge graph retrieval that replaces uniform decay with a continuous decay surface parameterized by velocity and volatility, learning domain-level, context-level, and entity-level decay parameters from data.
AI.motivation: Existing temporal approaches apply uniform decay using a single forgetting curve, treating all facts as equally current regardless of knowledge type, which is fundamentally misspecified since different knowledge types exhibit different temporal dynamics.
AI.method: The framework uses a continuous decay surface with two orthogonal signals (velocity and volatility), decomposed into three learnable levels (domain, context, entity). Parameters are learned via survival analysis on observed value lifetimes, treating value supersession as the event of interest.
AI.result: Recovers planted hierarchical parameters perfectly (HDBSCAN ARI=1.0); validates on 107 Wikipedia articles and 1,163 patient records; demonstrates natural emergence of velocity-volatility clusters and universal Lindy effect (Weibull k<1); uniform decay performs 18x worse than no temporal weighting.
AI.conclusion: Heterogeneous decay significantly outperforms uniform decay, with each hierarchy level contributing measurable improvement, and the approach requires no predefined taxonomies or domain expertise.

title: Better Models, Faster Training: Sigmoid Attention for single-cell Foundation Models
summary: Training stable biological foundation models requires rethinking attention mechanisms: we find that using sigmoid attention as a drop in replacement for softmax attention a) produces better learned representations: on six diverse single-cell datasets, sigmoid achieves 25% higher cell-type separation, better cell-type cohesion metrics, and lower validation loss, b) faster training, models with sigmoid attention train up to 10% faster than their softmax counterparts, and c) more stable training by eliminating inherent sources of instability in softmax attention. We establish that sigmoid attention has globally bounded derivatives ($\leq 0.25$) as opposed to softmax, and a diagonal Jacobian structure in contrast with softmax's dense coupling, which together help alleviate training instabilities. In stress tests on 160M-parameter bidirectional attention models trained without gradient clipping on 8K-token sequences, softmax diverges catastrophically, with gradients exploding by four orders of magnitude, while sigmoid remains stable. Finally, we implement and open-source TritonSigmoid, an efficient GPU kernel that achieves 515 TFLOPS on H100 GPUs, outperforming both FlashAttention-2 and FlashSigmoid, with native padding support, which is essential for biological sequences. Our results establish sigmoid attention as both theoretically grounded and empirically superior for biological foundation models. Code is available at https://github.com/MSDLLCpapers/triton-sigmoid

AI.tldr: Sigmoid attention outperforms softmax attention for biological foundation models, achieving better cell-type separation (25% higher), faster training (up to 10%), and more stable training due to globally bounded derivatives and diagonal Jacobian structure. The authors also release TritonSigmoid, an efficient GPU kernel achieving 515 TFLOPS on H100 GPUs.
AI.motivation: Training stable biological foundation models requires rethinking attention mechanisms because softmax attention has inherent sources of instability (unbounded derivatives, dense coupling) that lead to catastrophic divergence in large-scale models trained on biological sequences.
AI.method: The authors replace softmax attention with sigmoid attention as a drop-in replacement, analyze its theoretical properties (globally bounded derivatives ≤ 0.25, diagonal Jacobian structure), and implement TritonSigmoid, an efficient GPU kernel supporting native padding.
AI.result: On six diverse single-cell datasets, sigmoid achieves 25% higher cell-type separation, better cell-type cohesion metrics, lower validation loss, up to 10% faster training, and stable training on 160M-parameter models with 8K-token sequences without gradient clipping, while softmax diverges catastrophically. TritonSigmoid achieves 515 TFLOPS on H100 GPUs.
AI.conclusion: Sigmoid attention is both theoretically grounded and empirically superior for biological foundation models, offering better representations, faster and more stable training compared to softmax attention.

title: Universal Nano-Bead Emitter Inks for Programmable Nanometric Fluorescent Architectures
summary: Fabricating brightly fluorescent layers with nanometric thickness and digitally controlled lateral structuration remains a challenge for next-generation photonic devices, optical calibration standards, and biocompatible interfaces. Here, we introduce Nano-Bead Emitters (NBEs), hydrogel nanoparticles covalently functionalized with fluorophores, as a universal, water-processable ink platform for fabricating programmable nanometric fluorescent architectures. By immobilizing fluorophores within a charged nanohydrogel scaffold, the platform entirely decouples film morphology from dye solubility. This molecule-independent strategy enables spectrally distinct, inherently water-insoluble dyes to be processed using a single, standardized aqueous ink formulation. Combined with laser-induced forward transfer (LIFT) printing, this additive approach yields highly uniform fluorescent layers (~7 nm thickness, sub-nanometric roughness). This structural invariance produces complex multicolor patterns sharing identical thickness and surface morphology across all spectral channels, a critical requirement for quantitative optical calibration. Furthermore, LIFT printing provides programmable, layer-by-layer control over fluorescence intensity via successive deposition cycles, yielding precisely tunable brightness without aggregation-caused quenching. This maskless technique enables rapid, high-fidelity printing of both monochromatic and multicolor patterns over macroscopic areas with absolute spatial resolution. Finally, these universally compatible NBE inks stably deposit onto diverse substrates (glass, polymers, semiconductors, metasurfaces), effectively bridging scalable manufacturing with high-performance integrated photonic systems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Nano-Bead Emitters (NBEs)—hydrogel nanoparticles with covalently attached fluorophores—as a universal, water-processable ink platform for fabricating nanometric fluorescent patterns. Combined with laser-induced forward transfer (LIFT) printing, NBEs enable highly uniform (~7 nm thick, sub-nanometer roughness), multicolor fluorescent layers with programmable brightness, no aggregation quenching, and compatibility with diverse substrates.
AI.motivation: Fabricating brightly fluorescent layers with nanometric thickness and digitally controlled lateral structuration remains a challenge for photonic devices, optical calibration standards, and biocompatible interfaces. Existing methods struggle with thickness uniformity, multicolor integration, and dye solubility issues.
AI.method: The authors develop Nano-Bead Emitters (NBEs)—hydrogel nanoparticles covalently functionalized with fluorophores. This decouples film morphology from dye solubility. They combine NBEs with laser-induced forward transfer (LIFT) printing for additive, maskless, programmable deposition.
AI.result: NBEs yield highly uniform fluorescent layers (~7 nm thickness, sub-nanometric roughness). The platform enables multicolor patterns with identical thickness and surface morphology across channels. LIFT printing provides tunable brightness via successive deposition without aggregation-caused quenching, achieving high-fidelity monochromatic and multicolor patterns over macroscopic areas with absolute spatial resolution. NBEs deposit stably onto diverse substrates (glass, polymers, semiconductors, metasurfaces).
AI.conclusion: The NBE ink platform, combined with LIFT printing, offers a universal, scalable solution for creating programmable nanometric fluorescent architectures, effectively bridging scalable manufacturing with high-performance integrated photonic systems.

title: T-cell repertoire response in individuals with post-acute sequelae of COVID-19
summary: T-cells are central to SARS-CoV-2 clearance and immunological memory, yet their contribution to the persistence of post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC) remains poorly understood. The immunological features that distinguish individuals who develop PASC from those who recover fully are unresolved, in part due to the phenotypic heterogeneity of the condition and the likely multiplicity of its underlying mechanisms. Here, we profiled longitudinal bulk TCR$β$ repertoires from 120 individuals in the INCOV cohort--71 with PASC and 49 without--sampled at two to three time points spanning the acute and post-acute phases of infection. Using robust statistical modeling of repertoire composition and clonal dynamics, we found that global statistics such as V, J gene usage and CDR3 length do not differ between groups, but that locally enriched sequence motifs and differentially dynamic clones reveal distinct T-cell signatures associated with PASC status. Clones contracting following the peak of the acute response were significantly enriched for SARS-CoV-2 specificity in both groups. Interestingly, Influenza A-specific TCRs were disproportionately enriched among contracting clones in PASC{$^+$} repertoires, implicating viral co-infection as a potential contributor to early disease severity and, possibly, PASC pathogenesis. Rare public TCR clones were markedly enriched for SARS-CoV-2 specificity, with PASC{$^+$} individuals harboring a modestly but significantly higher proportion than PASC{$^-$} individuals. Together, we identified over 1,000 candidate TCR$β$ receptors potentially discriminating PASC{$^+$} from PASC{$^-$} immune responses, opening a path toward the identification of disease-relevant T-cell specificities and the development of T-cell-based immunological biomarkers for long COVID.

AI.tldr: This study profiled TCRβ repertoires from 120 COVID-19 patients (71 with PASC/long COVID, 49 without) across acute and post-acute phases. While global repertoire statistics didn't differ between groups, locally enriched sequence motifs and clonal dynamics revealed distinct T-cell signatures. Notably, Influenza A-specific TCRs were enriched among contracting clones in PASC patients, suggesting viral co-infection may contribute to long COVID pathogenesis. Over 1,000 candidate TCRβ receptors were identified as potential discriminators between PASC and recovery.
AI.motivation: To understand the T-cell immunological features that distinguish individuals who develop post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC/long COVID) from those who fully recover, addressing the unresolved mechanisms underlying PASC heterogeneity.
AI.method: Longitudinal bulk TCRβ repertoire profiling from 120 individuals (INCOV cohort) across 2-3 time points spanning acute and post-acute phases, using robust statistical modeling of repertoire composition and clonal dynamics, including analysis of V/J gene usage, CDR3 length, sequence motifs, and clonal contraction/expansion patterns.
AI.result: Global statistics (V/J gene usage, CDR3 length) didn't differ between PASC+ and PASC- groups. However, locally enriched sequence motifs and differentially dynamic clones distinguished the groups. Influenza A-specific TCRs were disproportionately enriched among contracting clones in PASC+ repertoires, implicating viral co-infection. Rare public TCR clones showed enhanced SARS-CoV-2 specificity in PASC+ individuals. Over 1,000 candidate TCRβ receptors were identified.
AI.conclusion: Distinct T-cell dynamics signatures exist between PASC and recovered individuals, with viral co-infection (particularly Influenza A) potentially contributing to PASC pathogenesis. The identified candidate TCRβ receptors open a path toward T-cell-based immunological biomarkers for long COVID.

title: Entropy-Dominated Temporal Vocal Dynamics as Digital Biomarkers for Depression Detection
summary: Automated depression detection often relies on static aggregation of conversational signals, potentially obscuring clinically meaningful behavioral dynamics. We investigated whether entropy-driven temporal biomarkers improve depression detection beyond standard pooled features using the DAIC-WOZ corpus. Using 142 labeled participants, we reconstructed utterance-level acoustic trajectories and compared pooled temporal baselines, trajectory dynamics, Shannon entropy biomarkers, recurrence quantification, sample entropy, fractal complexity, and coupling biomarkers under leakage-aware validation. Static pooling achieved an AUC of 0.593, trajectory dynamics improved performance to 0.637, and entropy biomarkers produced the strongest statistically significant improvement over pooled baselines (AUC 0.646; nested cross-validated AUC 0.615; permutation p = 0.017). Entropy biomarkers outperformed recurrence, coupling, sample entropy, and fractalbased features, with several biomarkers stable across folds. These findings suggest depression-related signal may lie less in average acoustic levels than in entropy of conversational dynamics, supporting temporally informed digital phenotypes for mental-health assessment.

AI.tldr: This study shows entropy-based temporal biomarkers from conversational dynamics outperform standard pooled acoustic features for automated depression detection, achieving AUC 0.646 vs 0.593.
AI.motivation: Static aggregation of conversational signals may obscure clinically meaningful behavioral dynamics in depression detection.
AI.method: Used DAIC-WOZ corpus (142 participants), reconstructed utterance-level acoustic trajectories, compared pooled baselines, trajectory dynamics, Shannon entropy, recurrence quantification, sample entropy, fractal complexity, and coupling biomarkers under leakage-aware validation.
AI.result: Static pooling AUC=0.593, trajectory dynamics AUC=0.637, entropy biomarkers AUC=0.646 (nested CV AUC=0.615, permutation p=0.017). Entropy outperformed recurrence, coupling, sample entropy, and fractal features.
AI.conclusion: Depression-related signal lies more in entropy of conversational dynamics than average acoustic levels, supporting temporally informed digital phenotypes.

title: Modeling the impact of host diversity on the evolution of vector feeding preferences and implications for disease control
summary: Vector-borne diseases often infect multiple host species, increasing the likelihood of disease persistence due to the presence of multiple reservoirs. Vector biting patterns and feeding preferences can shift in response to selective pressures introduced by disease control interventions, altering the dynamics of transmission. In this paper, we develop a mathematical model that couples host diversity and adaptive vector behavior with vector-borne disease transmission dynamics, focusing on a system with two hosts and a vector population exhibiting preference for one host. We derive the basic reproduction number, $R_0$, a threshold that determines the existence of two equilibria in our model, and obtain conditions that can lead to the long-term persistence of the disease. Our analysis suggests that shortening the infectious period of the vector's preferred host is an effective control strategy. We also identified a threshold that determines whether shifting vector preference toward a non-preferred host amplifies or reduces the disease burden on the primary preferred host. Our results show that protective measures for the preferred host can trigger adaptive shifts in vector preferences, reducing disease prevalence in that host. However, this shift may lead to an increase in overall host prevalence.

AI.tldr: A mathematical model shows that adaptive vector biting preferences, driven by disease control interventions, can shift disease burden between host species, potentially reducing prevalence in preferred hosts but increasing overall host prevalence.
AI.motivation: Vector-borne diseases infect multiple host species, creating multiple reservoirs that increase disease persistence. Understanding how adaptive vector feeding preferences influence transmission dynamics is critical for effective control strategies.
AI.method: Developed a mathematical model coupling host diversity and adaptive vector behavior with disease transmission dynamics, analyzing a two-host system where vectors prefer one host, deriving the basic reproduction number $R_0$ and equilibrium conditions.
AI.result: Shortening the infectious period of the vector's preferred host is an effective control strategy. A threshold determines whether shifting preference toward a non-preferred host amplifies or reduces disease burden on the primary host. Protective measures for the preferred host can trigger adaptive shifts, reducing prevalence in that host but potentially increasing overall host prevalence.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive vector behavior complicates disease control; protecting the preferred host may paradoxically increase overall disease burden by shifting vectors to other hosts.

title: Epidemic Extinction in a Continuous SIRS Model with Vaccination
summary: Epidemics have shaped human history, often with devastating consequences, motivating the development of mathematical models to understand and control their dynamics. Among the many aspects of epidemic behavior, the conditions that lead to epidemic extinction stand out as a central-if not the fundamental-question in epidemic modeling. In this work, we study epidemic extinction in a continuous SIRS (Susceptible-Infected-Recovered-Susceptible) model governed by a system of ordinary differential equations (ODEs). The model includes vaccination as a time-dependent process and considers the reinfection of recovered individuals through waning immunity. We analyze how different parameter regimes -- particularly infection, recovery, and immunity loss rates -- affect the persistence or extinction of the epidemic. Special attention is given to the limitations of continuous population models, in which the infected fraction can fall below the equivalent of a single individual, leading to nonphysical outcomes such as unrealistically long persistence or artificial secondary peaks. By comparing the continuous SIRS dynamics with expected real-world thresholds for extinction, we highlight the importance of incorporating stochasticity or discrete effects to accurately describe epidemic fade-out.

AI.tldr: This paper studies epidemic extinction in a continuous SIRS model with vaccination and waning immunity, highlighting how continuous ODE models can produce nonphysical outcomes (e.g., unrealistically long persistence) when infected fractions fall below single-individual thresholds, and argues for incorporating stochastic or discrete effects to accurately capture extinction.
AI.motivation: Epidemics have historically caused devastating consequences, driving the need for mathematical models to understand their dynamics. The fundamental question of what leads to epidemic extinction motivates this work.
AI.method: The authors analyze a continuous SIRS model governed by ODEs, incorporating time-dependent vaccination and waning immunity (reinfection). They examine various parameter regimes including infection, recovery, and immunity loss rates.
AI.result: Continuous population models have limitations: the infected fraction can fall below the equivalent of a single individual, leading to nonphysical outcomes such as unrealistically long persistence or artificial secondary peaks.
AI.conclusion: Comparing continuous SIRS dynamics with real-world extinction thresholds highlights the necessity of incorporating stochasticity or discrete effects to accurately describe epidemic fade-out.

title: Personalizing Cancer Models under Data Scarcity via Parameter Decomposition
summary: Personalized cancer modeling for clinical applications requires robust and efficient parameter calibration, particularly in settings with limited patient data. This need is especially critical for medical digital twins (MDTs), which are virtual representations of disease continuously updated using longitudinal patient measurements. In this work, we propose a novel parameter personalization framework for dynamical cancer models under data scarcity. Our approach decomposes selected model parameters into a common component, shared across patients, and a personalized component, which is patient-specific and can be updated as new data become available. The common component captures population-level structure and is estimated once, providing an informed prior that enables rapid and accurate personalization. We demonstrate the effectiveness of this framework using synthetic data generated from canonical dynamical systems, such as logistic growth models with optimized treatment interventions. Our results show that parameter decomposition significantly improves calibration performance in limited-data regimes, facilitating fast and reliable personalization and supporting the development of patient-specific cancer models and MDTs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework that decomposes model parameters into common (shared across patients) and personalized (patient-specific) components to enable rapid and accurate calibration of dynamical cancer models under data scarcity, supporting medical digital twin development.
AI.motivation: Personalized cancer modeling requires robust parameter calibration with limited patient data, which is critical for medical digital twins (MDTs) that need continuous updates from sparse longitudinal measurements.
AI.method: Parameters are decomposed into a common component (population-level structure, estimated once) and a personalized component (patient-specific, updated as new data arrives). The common component serves as an informed prior for personalization.
AI.result: Using synthetic data from logistic growth models with optimized treatment interventions, the parameter decomposition framework significantly improved calibration performance in limited-data regimes, enabling fast and reliable personalization.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively supports patient-specific cancer model development and medical digital twins by facilitating rapid, accurate personalization even when patient data is scarce.

title: Incorporating the underuse problem in the tragedy of the commons
summary: The tragedy of the commons has traditionally been framed as a problem of resource overuse driven by self-interested exploitation. In contrast, growing empirical evidence shows that insufficient use or abandonment of natural resources, known as underuse, can also lead to ecological degradation and loss of ecosystem services. Despite its relevance, underuse has rarely been examined within evolutionary theories of resource use. Here, we develop a simple eco-evolutionary model that integrates both provisioning and non-provisioning ecosystem services to analyze the evolution of resource-use strategies. Using adaptive dynamics, we investigate how individual resource use evolves while altering resource abundance. The model shows that overuse and underuse arise naturally as alternative evolutionary outcomes of the same underlying process, alongside intermediate use and evolutionary branching. We derive analytical conditions for the existence, number, and stability of evolutionarily singular strategies, and show that the qualitative evolutionary fate is primarily determined by the shape of provisioning benefits. Only when provisioning benefits increase in a concave manner does evolutionary dynamics converge to a unique intermediate strategy that is continuously stable. In contrast, convex increasing benefits generate a broader range of outcomes: overuse, underuse, bi-stability, and evolutionary branching. By explicitly comparing the continuously stable strategy with the socially optimal strategy, we further quantify how their deviations depend on the valuation of non-provisioning services. Our results provide a theoretical framework for viewing the common-pool resource dilemmas as intrinsically two-sided evolutionary problems, and offer a baseline for future studies exploring interventions to address overuse and underuse simultaneously.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an eco-evolutionary model showing that both overuse AND underuse of common-pool resources can emerge as alternative evolutionary outcomes, depending on the shape of provisioning benefits (concave vs. convex).
AI.motivation: Traditional tragedy of the commons focuses on overuse, but growing evidence shows underuse (resource abandonment) also causes ecological degradation. The authors aim to integrate both phenomena into a unified evolutionary framework.
AI.method: The authors develop a simple eco-evolutionary model incorporating provisioning and non-provisioning ecosystem services, analyzed using adaptive dynamics to study evolution of resource-use strategies under changing resource abundance.
AI.result: Concave provisioning benefits → unique stable intermediate strategy. Convex benefits → multiple outcomes: overuse, underuse, bi-stability, and evolutionary branching. The deviation from socially optimal strategy depends on valuation of non-provisioning services.
AI.conclusion: Common-pool resource dilemmas are intrinsically two-sided evolutionary problems (overuse and underuse). The framework provides a baseline for interventions addressing both extremes simultaneously.

title: Simulating Infant First-Person Sensorimotor Experience via Motion Retargeting from Babies to Humanoids
comment: Submitted to IEEE ICDL. 8 pages, 6 figures
summary: Motion retargeting from humans to human-like artificial agents is becoming increasingly important as humanoid robots grow more capable. However, most existing approaches focus only on reproducing kinematics and ignore the rich sensorimotor experience associated with human movement. In this work, we present a framework for simulating the multimodal sensorimotor experiences of infants using physical and virtual humanoids. From a single video, our method reconstructs the infant's body configuration by extracting its skeletal structure and estimating the full 3D pose from each frame. Then we map the reconstructed motion onto several developmental platforms: the physical iCub robot and the virtual simulators pyCub, EMFANT and MIMo. Replaying the retargeted motions on these embodiments produces simulated multisensory streams including proprioception (joints and muscles), touch, and vision.
