For the best-matching embodiment, the retargeting achieves sub-centimeter accuracy and enables a rich multimodal analysis of infant development as well as enhanced automated annotation of behaviors. This framework provides a unique window into the infant's sensorimotor experience, offering new tools for robotics, developmental science, and early detection of neurodevelopmental disorders. The code is available at https://github.com/ctu-vras/motion-retargeting/.

AI.tldr: A framework that reconstructs infant motion from video and retargets it onto physical/virtual humanoid platforms (iCub, pyCub, EMFANT, MIMo) to simulate multimodal sensorimotor experiences (proprioception, touch, vision), achieving sub-centimeter accuracy for developmental science and robotics applications.
AI.motivation: Existing motion retargeting approaches focus only on kinematics, ignoring the rich sensorimotor experiences associated with human movement, especially for studying infant development.
AI.method: Extract infant skeletal structure and 3D pose from a single video, then retarget the motion onto four platforms (physical iCub robot, virtual pyCub, EMFANT, MIMo) to generate simulated multisensory streams including proprioception, touch, and vision.
AI.result: Sub-centimeter retargeting accuracy for the best-matching embodiment; enables rich multimodal analysis of infant development and enhanced automated behavior annotation.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a unique window into infant sensorimotor experience, offering new tools for robotics, developmental science, and early detection of neurodevelopmental disorders.

title: Benchmarking virtual cell models for in-the-wild perturbation response
summary: Virtual cell (VC) models aim to predict cellular responses to any perturbations in silico and have emerged as a promising approach for drug discovery and precision medicine. Yet, a clear gap still remains: while models routinely reported impressive results on standard benchmarks, it is unclear whether their predictions are truly meaningful in practice. This is mainly due to limitations in current evaluation setups, which are often overly simplified or inconsistent, and do not reflect the complexity and variability of real biological systems. Here, we introduce a standardized and modular benchmarking framework for virtual cell prediction. Our framework evaluates diverse models under in-the-wild challenging scenarios, including unseen cell contexts, unseen perturbations, and cross-dataset generalization, which better reflect practical applications. Our analysis shows that model performance is highly context-dependent and shaped by task design and evaluation criteria. In commonly used setups, performance is often overestimated, and naive dataset aggregation can even reduce performance. When evaluated under more strict conditions, model performance drops markedly, indicating limited robustness to shifts across cellular contexts. In unseen perturbation settings, models including simple linear approaches capture global transcriptional trends but fail to recover fine-grained perturbation-specific effects. In addition, different evaluation metrics focus on different biological properties, leading to substantially different model rankings. Together, our framework provides a more reliable and biologically grounded evaluation, offering clearer guidance for applying virtual cell models in real scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a standardized benchmarking framework for virtual cell models, revealing that current evaluations overestimate performance and that models struggle with real-world challenges like unseen cell contexts, perturbations, and cross-dataset generalization, with performance being highly context-dependent and metric-sensitive.
AI.motivation: While VC models show impressive results on standard benchmarks, it is unclear whether their predictions are truly meaningful in practice due to overly simplified or inconsistent evaluation setups that don't reflect real biological complexity.
AI.method: The authors developed a standardized, modular benchmarking framework that evaluates VC models under in-the-wild challenging scenarios: unseen cell contexts, unseen perturbations, and cross-dataset generalization, using multiple evaluation metrics.
AI.result: Model performance is highly context-dependent; overestimated in common setups; naive data aggregation harms performance; strict conditions cause marked performance drops; simple linear models capture global trends but miss fine-grained perturbation-specific effects; different metrics lead to substantially different model rankings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides more reliable, biologically grounded evaluation, offering clearer guidance for applying virtual cell models in real-world scenarios.

title: On Agentic Behavioral Modeling
summary: Integrating theoretical neuroscience, decision theory, and probabilistic inference offers a promising route to understanding human cognition, yet concrete methodological bridges between agentic AI models and behavioral data analysis remain formally underdeveloped. We advance this synthesis under the framework of agentic behavioral modeling (ABM), which treats artificial agents as latent, generative hypotheses about cognitive mechanisms and evaluates them by their statistical adequacy in explaining human behavior. After outlining its conceptual foundations, we apply the framework to two minimal laboratory paradigms: a binary perceptual contrast-discrimination task and a symmetric two-armed bandit learning task. We formalize each task-agent-data system as a joint probability model, derive explicit conditional log-likelihoods for behavioral inference, validate different model variants using model and parameter recovery simulations, and evaluate them in light of empirical data. Using these minimal examples, we provide an agent-centric interpretation of the psychometric function, derive optimal policies for both tasks, and show the equivalence between Rescorla-Wagner learning and Bayesian inference in symmetric bandits. More broadly, this work may serve as a conceptual and practical foundation for applying ABM to cognitive behavioral science.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Agentic Behavioral Modeling (ABM), a framework that bridges AI agents and human behavior analysis by treating agents as generative hypotheses about cognition, validated through two minimal lab tasks (perceptual discrimination and bandit learning).
AI.motivation: To develop formal methodological bridges between agentic AI models and behavioral data analysis, which remain underdeveloped despite theoretical promise.
AI.method: ABM framework: treat artificial agents as latent generative hypotheses; formalize task-agent-data as joint probability models; derive conditional log-likelihoods; validate via model/parameter recovery simulations and empirical evaluation.
AI.result: Agent-centric interpretation of psychometric function; optimal policies for both tasks; equivalence between Rescorla-Wagner learning and Bayesian inference in symmetric bandits.
AI.conclusion: Provides a conceptual and practical foundation for applying ABM to cognitive behavioral science.

title: Complex Effects of Salt on Small-Angle X-ray Scattering of BSA Originate From the Interplay of Ions and Hydration Water
summary: Salts are an integral part of the environment for living systems and, therefore, understanding their effects on proteins and other biomolecules is of fundamental interest. Small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) of protein solutions can provide valuable information on salt effects, but extracting this information has been a significant challenge. For example, SAXS data of bovine serum albumin (BSA) at various salt concentrations were fit to three different spherical models. Here we combined the newly developed FMAPIq approach with explicit-solvent all-atom molecular dynamics simulations to show that the complex effects of salt on the SAXS of BSA originate from the interplay of ions and hydration water, leading to a general picture of protein-ion-water interactions.

AI.tldr: This paper uses a combined FMAPIq and molecular dynamics approach to reveal how salt effects on BSA SAXS data originate from ion-hydration water interplay, providing a general model of protein-ion-water interactions.
AI.motivation: Understanding salt effects on proteins is fundamentally important since salts are ubiquitous in biological environments, but extracting meaningful information from SAXS data has been challenging.
AI.method: The authors combined the FMAPIq approach with explicit-solvent all-atom molecular dynamics simulations to analyze SAXS data of BSA at various salt concentrations.
AI.result: The complex salt effects on BSA SAXS patterns arise from the interplay between ions and hydration water, not simply from protein structural changes.
AI.conclusion: A general picture of protein-ion-water interactions emerges, explaining how salts influence SAXS profiles through the coupled behavior of ions and water molecules at the protein surface.

title: Multisensory learning recruits visual neurons into an olfactory memory engram
summary: Associating multiple sensory cues with a single experience or object is a fundamental process that improves object recognition and memory performance. However, neural mechanisms that bind sensory features during learning and augment memory expression are unknown. Here we demonstrate multisensory appetitive and aversive memory in Drosophila. Combining colours and odours improved memory performance, even when each sensory modality was tested alone. Temporal control of neuronal function revealed visually-selective mushroom body Kenyon Cells (KCs) to be required for enhancement of visual and olfactory memory recall after multisensory training. Synapse-level connectomics suggests that valence-relevant dopaminergic reinforcement could permit the KC-spanning serotonergic DPM neurons to bridge between previously modality-selective KC streams. Consistent with this model, DPM transmission is uniquely required during multisensory memory formation and for enhanced expression of olfactory memory afterwards. In addition, signalling via the DopR1 dopamine receptor is required in APL neurons, suggesting that reinforcing dopamine could locally release GABA-ergic inhibition to permit bridging microcircuits to function. Cross-modal binding thereby expands the KCs representing the olfactory memory engram into those representing the colour. We propose that broadening of the engram improves memory performance after multisensory learning and permits a single sensory feature to retrieve the memory of the multimodal experience.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates multisensory memory binding in Drosophila, showing that combining colors and odors improves memory performance. The study reveals neural mechanisms involving mushroom body Kenyon Cells, serotonergic DPM neurons, and dopaminergic signaling that enable cross-modal memory formation and retrieval.
AI.motivation: To understand the neural mechanisms that bind multiple sensory features during learning and enhance memory expression, a fundamental yet poorly understood process in neuroscience.
AI.method: The authors used Drosophila with multisensory (color + odor) appetitive and aversive conditioning, combined with temporal control of neuronal function, synapse-level connectomics, and targeted genetic manipulations to dissect the neural circuits involved in cross-modal memory binding.
AI.result: Multisensory training improved memory performance for both modalities tested alone. Visually-selective mushroom body Kenyon Cells were required for enhancement. DPM neuron transmission was uniquely required during multisensory memory formation, and DopR1 receptor signaling in APL neurons was necessary. The cross-modal binding expands the memory engram to include Kenyon Cells representing both modalities.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal sensory binding broadens the memory engram representation, allowing a single sensory feature to retrieve the full multimodal memory. This engram expansion improves memory performance after multisensory learning.
